_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Lawrence County, PA. Formed 1849 from Beaver and Mercer counties. County seat at New Castle. The newest county in the cluster, formed during the canal/railroad transition era. Later became an industrial center with tin plate, steel, and ceramics. Good record survival from county formation.

previous_courthouses.fate: Lawrence County was carved from Beaver and Mercer counties. Court met in temporary quarters while the first purpose-built courthouse was constructed.
Brick courthouse. Served through the Civil War and the early industrial period. Replaced by the current Beaux-Arts building in 1901.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or catastrophic loss
record_losses.description: Lawrence County's courthouse has not experienced a catastrophic fire or natural disaster. Records run continuously from the county's 1849 formation. Pre-1849 records for the Lawrence County area are filed under Beaver County (south) and Mercer County (north). Because Lawrence County was formed relatively late, the pre-1849 records must be researched in two parent counties.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in the Lawrence County area before 1849, the records are split between Beaver and Mercer counties. After 1849, Lawrence County's own records are intact and continuous.
record_losses.source: Pennsylvania Historical and Museum Commission. Guide to the Record Groups in the Pennsylvania State Archives.

deeds.gaps: No known gaps. Pre-1849 deeds filed under Beaver County (for the southern portion) and Mercer County (for the northern portion).
deeds.repository: Lawrence County Recorder of Deeds, New Castle; Pennsylvania State Archives microfilm

wills_estates.gaps: Pre-1849 estates handled by Beaver and Mercer county probate courts.
wills_estates.repository: Lawrence County Register of Wills; Pennsylvania State Archives microfilm

marriages.completeness: Marriage licenses from 1885. Pre-1885 marriages through church records.
marriages.gaps: 36-year gap between county formation (1849) and marriage license law (1885) filled by church records. This is the shortest such gap in the cluster because Lawrence County was formed latest.
marriages.repository: Lawrence County Clerk of the Orphans' Court; Pennsylvania State Archives

court_minutes.repository: Lawrence County Prothonotary; Pennsylvania State Archives microfilm

vital_records.repository: Pennsylvania Department of Health; Pennsylvania State Archives

substitute_sources.source_type: Tin plate and ceramics company records
substitute_sources.description: New Castle became the 'Tin Plate Capital of the World' in the late nineteenth century. Shenango China, Shenango Pottery, and various tin plate works employed thousands. Company records — employment rolls, payroll, housing — document the industrial workforce.
substitute_sources.repository: Lawrence County Historical Society; University of Pittsburgh Archives; Pennsylvania State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Employment records for the tin plate and ceramics industries document immigrant and native-born workers. The ceramics industry drew skilled workers from specific regions in England (Staffordshire potters) and Italy.
substitute_sources.source: Lawrence County Historical Society. Collections and Finding Aids.

substitute_sources.description: Lawrence County appears as a separate enumeration from 1850. Manufacturing schedules document the county's industrial development.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential baseline. The 1850 census is the first to enumerate Lawrence County separately. Pre-1850 families appear in Beaver and Mercer county enumerations.

substitute_sources.source_type: Ethnic church records — Italian, Eastern European
substitute_sources.description: New Castle's industrial economy attracted Italian, Slovak, Polish, Croatian, and other Eastern European immigrants. Ethnic parishes maintained registers in their original languages.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual parishes; Diocese of Pittsburgh Archives; Lawrence County Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Parish registers document baptisms, marriages, and burials with village-of-origin information for immigrants. The Italian community in New Castle was one of the largest in western Pennsylvania outside Pittsburgh.
substitute_sources.source: Lawrence County Historical Society. Ethnic Heritage Collection.

narrative_hooks: Lawrence County was formed in 1849 — if {ancestor_name} lived in the New Castle area before that date, the records are split between Beaver County to the south and Mercer County to the north, and the researcher must check both parent counties.

If {ancestor_name} worked in the tin plate mills or the pottery works in New Castle, the company records may document {pronoun_possessive} employment when the county records show only a name in a census. New Castle was the Tin Plate Capital of the World, and its factories drew workers from across Europe.

New Castle's Italian community was one of the largest in western Pennsylvania — if {ancestor_name} was Italian, the Catholic parish records at the ethnic church may be the most detailed genealogical source, recording the village in Italy the census never asked about.

Pennsylvania's marriage license law started in 1885, just 36 years after Lawrence County was formed — the shortest pre-license gap in the cluster, which means church records fill a smaller period than in the older counties.
source_references: Pennsylvania Historical and Museum Commission. Guide to the Record Groups in the Pennsylvania State Archives.
Lawrence County Historical Society. Collections and Finding Aids.
Lawrence County Historical Society. Ethnic Heritage Collection.
